result.diagnosis: Turn 2 bolted one confirmed lever (+6.0 journal attribution) onto output machinery that was actively mutilating three dimensions, and shipped it without a single output-level lint. The specific kill chain: (1) THE SPLITTER AMPUTATES AT "et al." — cellcog_composer.py:391 `re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', p)` cuts every "Agrawal et al. report that X" into a head ("...Agrawal et al.") and a tail ("report that X"); proven by direct execution. The head passes the attribution regex and ships as a complete 'sentence'; the tail fails it, is misclassified as synthesis, and is either gate-dropped (74 drops) or shipped as a lowercase orphan. The turn-2 report contains 5 dangling "Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al." full-stop stumps, adjacent duplicated heads (line 59), orphan "show that..." fragments (line 121), and "**[Unresolved]**" as an ENTIRE subsection body (line 177). The judge quoted these amputations back verbatim: "duplicated clauses, incomplete sentences, missing words". (2) THE GATE IS A RACE CONDITION — `_SYNTH_CARDS` is a module global (:324) reassigned by 6 threads (:434-435, ThreadPoolExecutor at :441), so sentences were validated against a RANDOM other subsection's premises; drops were nondeterministic, whole subsections evaporated. (3) 27 STATELESS PARALLEL WRITERS + overlapping greedy `_select` re-narrated the same card up to 5x ("technical feasibility is not sufficient" 5x, "static model with fixed capital" 5x, Bresnahan QJE attribution 10x) — roughly 1,500-2,000 of 6,305 words are restatement, putting UNIQUE content near ~4.5k words, BELOW the measured 5,000w floor, with zero transitions because no writer ever saw neighboring text: that is the judge's "fragmented narrative... without adequate transitions", and it is why paragraph-size/H3 surgery moved readability -0.08 — we shortened the fragments and left them disconnected. (4) ONE MANDATED TEMPLATE (WRITE_PROMPT :295-298) stamped 135 times, plus "use this exact wording" propagating "the The Quarterly Journal of Economics" 15x — the judge's #1 readability complaint names "Writing in" explicitly. (5) THE 4IR SECTIONS DIED AT EXTRACTION, NOT IN THE CORPUS — 7 of the 70 corpus papers discuss 4IR/Industry 4.0, but EXTRACT_PROMPT (:102) asks only for labor-market FINDINGS, so 0 of 133 cards mention 4IR; `_select` then fed the '4IR differs from previous three' subsection cards about researcher attention-shifting (top lexical score: 2), and both 4IR subsections plus 5 others (7/28) silently vanished via `results.get(...,'')`. 4IR-named criteria scored 4.5/4.2/5.0 across all three content dimensions. (6) We DELETED the bibliography instead of testing a cleaner-proof format; the judge penalized its absence inside our one winning criterion ("lacks a formal reference list, making verification difficult" — capped at 7.5). Net arithmetic: +0.63 on one dimension against -0.58/-1.14/-0.08 on three. The lever was real — we beat the reference on journal-only (7.5 vs 4.0), English-only (9.0 vs 8.0), AND literature depth (7.0 vs 6.8) — but everything around it was an unlinted regression, and changing 5 things at once meant only the judge's written receipt made it diagnosable.

turn3_changes.change: Fix the amputating machinery: abbreviation-aware sentence splitter (protect 'et al.', 'e.g.', initials); pass cards into _clean(raw, sel) as a parameter and delete the _SYNTH_CARDS global (kill the 6-thread race); when a gate drops a tail, drop the WHOLE claim-unit including its attribution head — never ship a stump; if a subsection comes back empty, <120 words, or not ending in terminal punctuation, REGENERATE instead of skipping the heading.
turn3_changes.targets_criterion: L1 Language Clarity (-4.5: 'incomplete sentences, duplicated clauses, missing words'), S1 Structure (-4.0: 'incomplete sections, abrupt endings'), Value & Foresight (-4.2: 'truncated')
turn3_changes.expected: Removes every machine-made defect the judge quoted; readability 4.63 -> 6.5+ since the complaint list is almost entirely gate artifacts; restores the 7 evaporated subsections' headings-with-content

turn3_changes.change: Attribution variety while keeping the confirmed lever: 6-8 sanctioned in-prose shapes ('In a 2019 Journal of Economic Perspectives article, X show...', 'X's 2015 analysis in the Y finds...', 'A field experiment in Science by...'), max 2 'Writing in' per subsection, first-mention-full/subsequent-short ('Acemoglu and Restrepo further show'), strip doubled 'the The'. Update the evidence-sentence detector (:403) to key on corpus venue names instead of the literal 'Writing in the' token, or the gate will amputate the new variety.
turn3_changes.where: cellcog_composer.py WRITE_PROMPT :295-298 and _clean() attribution regex :403; verify each shape with /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/cleaner_survival_test.py BEFORE composing
turn3_changes.targets_criterion: L1 Language Clarity ('repeated phrases such as Writing in') while preserving Exclusive-Journal-Citation at 7.5+
turn3_changes.expected: Kills the named readability complaint without touching the +6.0 mechanism — cellcog itself has 131 journal mentions and 9+ readability, proving varied journal-in-prose is compatible with top clarity

turn3_changes.change: Restore a formal reference list in cleaner-proof form: '## References' with full journal-style entries (Authors (Year). Title. Journal, vol, pages, DOI) and ZERO [n] markers anywhere; empirically verify the block survives RACE's cleaner before shipping.
turn3_changes.where: cellcog_composer.py write_report() assembly :449-470; survival check via scripts/cleaner_survival_test.py pattern (the harness that measured 0/12 vs 10-12/12)
turn3_changes.targets_criterion: Exclusive Citation of High-Quality Journal Articles — the judge's stated residual at 7.5 is exactly 'lacks a formal reference list, making verification difficult'
turn3_changes.expected: 7.5 -> ~9 on the single criterion where we already beat the reference by +3.5; cheapest points on the board

turn3_changes.change: Second extraction pass with a broadened contract: in addition to findings, extract FRAMEWORK/DEFINITIONAL cards (what 4IR is, cyber-physical systems, GPT properties, explicit contrasts with the first three industrial revolutions) and SCALE/PROJECTION cards (Frey-Osborne 47%, adoption rates, robot density). The 7 corpus papers mentioning 4IR produced 0 of 133 cards because EXTRACT_PROMPT only asks for labor-market findings — the material exists and was never harvested.
turn3_changes.where: cellcog_composer.py EXTRACT_PROMPT :102-131; re-run --extract; outline sections 'AI as the Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' subsections
turn3_changes.targets_criterion: Grounding in 4IR (4.5), Insightful 4IR Integration (4.2), Integration of 4IR Theme (5.0), Exploration of Disruptive Character and Scale (5.5) — 4IR alone is named in criteria across all three content dimensions (~11.5%+ of rubric)
turn3_changes.expected: The two missing 4IR subsections get real cards and real prose; +2-3 points on four criteria that are currently our deepest holes

turn3_changes.change: Targeted corpus EXPANSION harvested from rank10's reference list — the ~15-20 journal-tier items (published QJE 2025 Brynjolfsson-Li-Raymond, Eloundou et al. Science, Felten SMJ, JSM tourism/hospitality, plus 3-5 sectoral journal papers for healthcare/finance/legal) — then re-extract. Do NOT import facts from rank10's T5/T6/UNKNOWN sources (Toptal, substack, HR Executive).
turn3_changes.where: /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/journal_corpus_fetch.py + outputs/journal_corpus_content.json (currently 70 papers: 34 CITATION_ONLY, 24 ABSTRACT_ONLY, only 12 FULLTEXT)

turn3_changes.expected: Recovers most of the -1.14 comprehensiveness collapse from inside the journal-only constraint; industry scope 5.2 -> 7+ with citeable sector evidence instead of one 2011 hospital-robotics paper

turn3_changes.change: Partition cards across subsections (each card fully narrated exactly ONCE; later references must be short back-references, enforced by a used-card ledger shared across writers) instead of greedy overlapping _select.
turn3_changes.targets_criterion: S1 Structure ('repetition'), Breadth of Dimensions (frees ~1,500-2,000 words of duplicate budget; unique content is currently ~4.5k words, under the measured 5,000w floor)

turn3_changes.change: Sequential cohesion pass AFTER assembly: for each H2, write a 2-3 sentence section lede, and rewrite the first sentence of each subsection as a bridge, with the joiner given the actual neighboring text. This targets the judge's stated defect (COHESION), not structure counts, which are a proven well-powered null (898-panel +0.002/SD; turn 2's H3 0->21 moved readability -0.08).

turn3_changes.expected: +2-3 on foresight; removes the 'abrupt ending' structural penalty at the document's most-read position

result.corpus_decision: COMPOSE from the journal corpus — but expand it by harvesting the journal-tier subset of rank10's sources, and treat rank10 as a SOURCE MAP, never as text to transform. The -1.14 collapse looks like an indictment of the corpus swap, but the per-criterion decomposition says otherwise: within comprehensiveness we BEAT the reference on Depth/Representativeness of Literature (7.0 vs 6.8) — the canonical-journal spine is a strength — while the collapse concentrated in Industry Scope (5.2 vs ref 8.0), Disruption Scale (5.5 vs 8.3), and 4IR Grounding (4.5 vs 8.5). Tracing each: the 4IR hole is an EXTRACTION bug (7 corpus papers discuss 4IR; 0 cards were extracted because the prompt only asked for labor-market findings); a further chunk is the 7/28 subsections that silently evaporated in the gate/race; and ~1,500-2,000 words of the 6,305 were duplicate restatements, leaving unique content near ~4.5k — under the measured 5,000w comprehensiveness floor. So roughly half of the -1.14 is recoverable by fixing machinery alone, no corpus change needed. The remainder (industry breadth, scale numbers) needs sources — and rank10's own reference list contains ~15-20 genuine journal-tier items (QJE, Science, SMJ, JEP, JSM, ETP) that cover exactly those gaps; add them to the corpus and re-extract. TRANSFORMING the rank10 report itself fails three ways: (1) its breadth is load-bearing on unciteable sources (Toptal, substack, HR Executive, WEF blogs) — visible attribution of those re-creates the 1.5/10 citation score we just escaped, and re-attributing their facts to journals they didn't come from is fabrication, which this pipeline exists to prevent; (2) its text is rubric poison that needs full rewrite anyway — it opens with meta-commentary ('This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above') and ends with a telemetry 'Limitations' section, both on the composer's own banned list, in 677-word paragraphs with 240 doomed [n] markers; (3) a full rewrite of 97-source text WITHOUT span-grounding reintroduces unverifiable-claim risk everywhere. The card pipeline is the faithfulness machinery and the attribution lever's substrate; keep it, feed it more journal papers, and fix the two stages (extraction contract, output gate) that actually destroyed comprehensiveness. Bonus: since R is not fixed (8.03 vs us, 7.36 vs cellcog), every recovered comprehensiveness point pays twice.
result.prose_defect_root_cause: ARCHITECTURE, with a prompt accessory and token budget a distant, real third. Primary — three code-level bugs in /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/scripts/cellcog_composer.py: (1) the sentence splitter `re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', p)` at :391 splits at every 'et al. ' — proven by direct execution ('...Agrawal et al.' | 'report that...') — and the downstream attribution test at :403 then misroutes the tail into the synthesis gate, which drops it (74 drops) or ships it as a lowercase orphan; the dangling head ships either way. Every defect the judge listed — 'duplicated clauses' (line 59's doubled head), 'incomplete sentences' (5x 'Damioli et al.' full-stop stumps at lines 131/133/141), 'missing words' (line 121's 'show that...' orphans), 'abrupt endings' (line 177's body = '**[Unresolved]**') — is mechanically this bug's signature, visible in the artifact. (2) `_SYNTH_CARDS` is a module global (:324) written by 6 concurrent threads (:434-435 under ThreadPoolExecutor :441), so the gate validated sentences against arbitrary other subsections' premises — nondeterministic amputation, emptied subsections. (3) Stateless parallel writers with overlapping greedy `_select` (:333) and no shared ledger guarantee both the repetition (same clause narrated up to 5x, same attribution line 10x) and the zero transitions ('fragmented narrative') — no writer ever saw a neighboring paragraph, so cohesion was architecturally impossible, which is precisely why the structure-only surgery (677w->106w paragraphs, 21 H3s) moved readability -0.08. Secondary — PROMPT: WRITE_PROMPT :295-298 mandates ONE exact attribution shape ('Writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>...'), stamped 135 times, plus 'use this exact wording' propagating 'the The Quarterly Journal of Economics' 15x; that is the judge's named phrase-repetition complaint, fixable in the prompt alone. Minor — TOKEN BUDGET: one paragraph ends mid-sentence ('...requires fundamental process and', line 189), a generation-truncation signature the gate cannot produce (it only removes whole splitter-delimited sentences); max_tokens=3000 with glm-5.2 needs a finish_reason check and headroom, but fixing budget alone would leave every other defect intact. The proof of allocation: the judge's complaint list maps item-for-item onto gate artifacts, not onto anything the model was asked and failed to do.
cheap_tests: Splitter unit test (fix 1): feed 'Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al. report that X.' through the new splitter — must return exactly 1 sentence; regression-run the 8 known amputation sites from turn 2 (report.md lines 59, 101, 121, 131, 133, 141, 171, 189). Runs in milliseconds, no LLM.

Stump/orphan lint on the assembled report (fix 1, build-fails on any hit): `grep -E 'et al\.\s+(However|These|Writing|\*\*\[|$)'` -> 0; `grep -E '\.\s+(show|report|find|note)s? that'` -> 0; `grep -c 'the The '` -> 0; no paragraph consisting solely of an epistemic label; every subsection's last character in .!?

Determinism test (fix 1, race): compose twice with cached/fixture LLM responses — the dropped-sentence lists must be byte-identical (impossible with the global; today's code fails this by construction). Code-level fallback: `grep -n 'global _SYNTH_CARDS' scripts/cellcog_composer.py` must return nothing.

Template histogram (fix 2): count each attribution shape per subsection (max 2 of any one shape), total 'Writing in' <= 25 document-wide, and no 12-gram shared across 2+ subsections — this catches both the 135-stamp monoculture and the 5x-restatement repetition with one python script, no LLM call.

Cleaner-survival re-run (fixes 2+3): scripts/cleaner_survival_test.py on the 6-8 new attribution shapes AND the new marker-free '## References' block through the PRODUCTION RACE cleaner — require journal-name survival >=10/12 per shape and the references section intact. This is the exact harness that measured 0/12 vs 10-12/12; ~1 minute of LLM cost versus a wasted compose+judge cycle.

Completeness gate (fixes 1+8): all 28 OUTLINE subsections present in the md, each >=120 words; every LLM response's finish_reason == 'stop' (assert in llm(), retry on 'length'); Implications+Conclusion >= 600 words combined.

4IR coverage probe (fixes 4+5): after re-extraction, assert >=8 evidence cards whose claim/span mentions 4IR/Industry 4.0/cyber-physical (currently 0 of 133); on the report, >=12 such mentions spread across >=5 H2 sections plus >=1 explicit contrast with prior industrial revolutions (grep 'steam|electricit|third industrial'). Card-level check runs before any composing.

Breadth proxy (fixes 5+6): distinct journal names in prose >= 25; each sectoral H3 contains its own sector terms (healthcare section mentions health/hospital, etc.); unique-content word count after 12-gram dedup >= 7,000 (turn 2's raw 6,305 concealed ~4.5k unique — below the 5,000w floor); card-usage ledger asserts every card narrated at most once.

findings: Q1 — ATTRIBUTION TEMPLATES: there is effectively ONE template, fired 135 times in 6,305 body words (one every 46.7 words, i.e. roughly every second sentence). Only three surface shapes exist, and they are the same template inverted: (A) 'Writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, <AUTHORS> ...' x91; (B) '<AUTHORS>, writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, ...' x37; (C) '; writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, <AUTHORS> ...' x6. The verb slot is two words: 'show(s) that' x79 + 'report(s) that' x40 = 119/135 (88%). 44 of 65 paragraphs carry 2+ attributions; 3 carry four. This is by construction: WRITE_PROMPT rule 1 (cellcog_composer.py:295-297) hard-codes exactly those two shapes and says 'in this exact shape', and _fmt_cards() (line 350) re-injects the literal string with 'ATTRIBUTION (use this exact wording)'. Variety was designed out.

Q1b — The template also bakes in mechanical grammar errors, because the attribution string is 'Writing in the {venue}' and the venue often already contains an article or is a bare proper name: 'the The Quarterly Journal of Economics' x15, 'the The Journal of Economic History' x1, 'in the Science in 2023' x5, 'in the PLOS ONE in 2020' x7, 'in the Sustainability in 2019'. Plus 'as Writing in the...' (capital W mid-sentence, L17), '; Writing in the...' (L19), and 'Bloss ... report that' (plural verb, single author, L143 x2).

Q2 — TRUNCATION, MECHANISM 1: max_tokens=3000 (cellcog_composer.py:429) IS DEAD CODE. openrouter_client.py:1966-1970 silently raises it to a GLM-5 floor: `_min_tokens = int(os.getenv('PG_GLM5_MIN_MAX_TOKENS', '4096')); if body.get('max_tokens', 0) < _min_tokens: body['max_tokens'] = _min_tokens`. The cost ledger (logs/pg_cost_ledger.jsonl) for the exact run that produced report.md — 28 calls from 17:37:37 to 17:42:11, matching report.md's 17:42:11.56 mtime and the 28 OUTLINE subsections — shows 22/28 calls returned MORE than 3000 output tokens, and 9/28 pinned at EXACTLY 4096 = cut off at the ceiling. _clean() has no terminal-punctuation check, so it emits the guillotined tail verbatim.

Q2b — TRUNCATION, MECHANISM 2 (the dominant one): _clean()'s sentence splitter at cellcog_composer.py:391, `sents = re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', p)`, TREATS THE PERIOD IN 'et al.' AS A SENTENCE BOUNDARY. 64 of 133 evidence cards (48%) carry an attribution ending in 'et al.'. Template A ('Writing in the <J> in <Y>, <A> show that <finding>.') therefore bisects into: half-1 = 'Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al.' which CONTAINS 'Writing in the' so line 403 bypasses the gate and KEEPS it; half-2 = 'show that <finding>.' which names no source and is >8 words, so it is routed to _gate_synthesis() and is frequently DROPPED. 42 bisection sites exist in the report. Verified by direct replay of the regex.

Q2c — The visible damage from the et-al. split: 4 HEADLESS ATTRIBUTIONS (finding deleted, stub survives) at L101, L131, L133, L141; 1 DOUBLED ATTRIBUTION at L59 (first finding-half gated out, both attribution-halves survived and were rejoined by `' '.join(good)` at line 410); 2 SUBJECTLESS PREDICATES at L121 (the attribution-halves were dropped and the bare verb-halves survived). Plus L177: an entire 'paragraph' that is nothing but the string '**[Unresolved]**'.

Q3 — SECTIONS THAT DO NOT FULFILL THEIR HEADINGS: the OUTLINE (cellcog_composer.py:233-282) plans 28 subsections; the report contains 21. SEVEN VANISHED SILENTLY. All 28 LLM calls SUCCEEDED (28 ledger rows, 0 with zero output, max duration 90s vs the 420s timeout), so the `except Exception: return job, '', []` path NEVER FIRED — the bodies were emptied by _clean() itself, and write_report():467-469 (`if not body: continue`) then dropped the heading with no warning, no retry, no error. GONE: 'How the Fourth Industrial Revolution differs from the previous three'; 'Why AI is a general-purpose technology rather than a single innovation'; 'Skill-biased technical change and its limits'; 'Exposure is not adoption, and adoption is not impact'; 'Evidence for reinstatement and new task creation'; 'Skill demand is being recomposed rather than simply raised'; 'Task-level productivity gains in controlled settings'.

Q3b — The deletions leave H2 headings promising content the body never delivers: '## Measuring Exposure, Adoption, and Realized Outcomes' lost its adoption/outcomes subsection (271w, 1 H3); '## Employment, Displacement, and Job Creation' lost job creation; '## Wages, Skills, and the Labor Share' lost skills; '## Productivity and the Generative-AI Turn' lost 'Task-level productivity gains in controlled settings' and now consists solely of 'Why large task gains coexist with weak aggregate effects' — it explains away gains it never presented; '## Theoretical Frameworks' lost SBTC.

Q3c — THINNEST / MOST UNFULFILLED SURVIVING SUBSECTIONS: (a) '### Where the literature genuinely disagrees' = 38 WORDS and names ZERO disagreements — one sentence about task reinstatement tagged **[Established finding]** (the opposite of a disagreement), then a paragraph that is the bare tag '**[Unresolved]**'. (b) '### Healthcare, education, and the caring professions' = 179w and contains ZERO healthcare, ZERO education-sector, ZERO care content — all four sentences are Frey & Osborne on '702 detailed occupations' and 'a Gaussian process classifier'. (c) '### Professional, financial, and knowledge services' = 214w and ends on a one-sentence paragraph about HOSPITAL LOGISTICS ROBOTS; nothing about finance. (d) '### What the evidence establishes' (173w) and '### What the evidence cannot yet resolve' (102w) are BOTH 100% Frey & Osborne restated four different ways. (e) '### Implications that follow from the mechanisms' = 135w, ONE paragraph, truncated mid-sentence.

Q4 — YES, THE 4IR SECTION IS THIN, AND WORSE: IT NEVER MENTIONS THE 4IR. '## AI as the Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' is 115 WORDS — 1 H3, 2 paragraphs — the thinnest section in the report by a factor of 2.4 (next-thinnest H2 is 271w; the mean is ~630w). Two of its three subsections were deleted, and they are precisely the two that were supposed to carry the 4IR argument. Across the entire body: 'Fourth Industrial Revolution' appears 2x — once in the HARD-CODED Abstract string (composer.py:451-453) and once as the H2 heading itself (emitted from the OUTLINE constant). ZERO occurrences in prose. 'steam' = 0. 'electricity'/'electrif' = 0. 'general-purpose' = 1 (the Abstract). There is no comparison to the previous three industrial revolutions anywhere in the document.

Q4b — The one surviving 4IR subsection does not fulfil ITS heading either. Heading: 'The scale and speed of the disruption claimed in the literature'. Body in full: para 1 is a single orphan sentence about Shrestha et al.'s FIVE CONTINGENCY FACTORS OF DECISION-MAKING (no scale, no speed — the word 'speed' occurs only as an item in Shrestha's list, a lexical coincidence); para 2 argues automation ISN'T large-scale. A section titled 'AI as the Driver of the Fourth Industrial Revolution' makes no claim about scale, no claim about speed, and no claim about the 4IR.

Q4c — WHY the 4IR subsections died: _select() (composer.py:333-342) scores cards by raw lexical overlap between the H3 TITLE's content words and the card's claim text. For 'How the Fourth Industrial Revolution differs from the previous three' the title words are {fourth, industrial, revolution, differs, from, previous, three}; the best card in the 133-card corpus scores 2, and no card discusses industrial revolutions at all. It therefore fed 10 near-irrelevant cards into a prompt that says 'these are the ONLY facts you may state'. The model had nothing to write with, and the gate deleted what it wrote. The bitter part: composer.py:229-231 contains a comment that PREDICTS this exact failure — "'4IR' secretly means 'compare AI to the previous three industrial revolutions' -- that reading is worth ~11.5% of the score across three separate criteria, and our current report drops it after paragraph one." The fix was designed into the OUTLINE and then silently deleted by _clean().

Q5 — NO REFERENCE LIST. The judge is right. report.md is 195 lines and the last line is body prose ('...the evidence does not establish whether educational attainment or wages drive computerisation risk.'). The file simply stops. Exhaustive grep: 'References' 0, 'REFERENCES' 0, 'Bibliography' 0, 'Works Cited' 0, 'doi.org' 0, 'DOI' 0, 'http' 0, 'vol.' 0, 'pp.' 0, '(2019)' 0, 'et al. (' 0. And there is NO CODE PATH that could produce one: the assembly block (composer.py:449-472) is a hard-coded title + 4-line Abstract, then `for sec, subs in OUTLINE`, then write. No references stanza exists. The evidence cards carry doi, authors, venue, year — every field a reference list needs — and none of it is ever emitted.

Q5b — It is worse than a missing list: the pipeline ACTIVELY DESTROYS every conventional citation. WRITE_PROMPT rule 1 (line 298) forbids parenthetical years, and _clean() line 408 (`good.append(PAREN_YEAR.sub('', s))`) DELETES any '(2019)' the model writes. So the report has zero bracket markers, zero author-year parentheticals, AND zero reference list. Every source exists ONLY as the repeated prose template — which is exactly the thing the judge flagged as a clarity defect. The 'visible attribution' strategy (docstring lines 9-13) was adopted to survive a downstream LLM cleaner and, in doing so, removed the report's entire citation apparatus.

Q5c — Corpus coverage is also short: 36 usable journal articles in the corpus, 32 produced evidence cards, but only 25 are ever cited. 7 carded papers appear nowhere in the report (van Esch et al., Chalmers et al., Sivathanu & Pillai, Baakeel, Kshetri, Fleming, Pashkevich et al.).

BONUS DEFECT — 44 epistemic labels across 65 paragraphs, and 7 of them are jammed INSIDE a clause where the verb should be, producing ungrammatical sentences the judge would read as broken: L81 'This convergence of industry-level observational and worker-level review evidence **[Established finding]** that routine tasks face negative long-run employment impacts' (NO VERB); L83 'whether firm-level business model change ultimately reverses macroeconomic displacement **[Unresolved]**.' (NO VERB); L105 'is **[Unresolved]** not settled by the current evidence'; L69/L71 'These findings jointly establish an **[Established finding]** that...'; L99 'these observational and theoretical works **[Established finding]** establish that...'; L103 'This indicates that **[Established finding]** labor demand outcomes...'.

BONUS — CONCURRENCY BUG that likely amplifies all of the above: composer.py:434-436 does `global _SYNTH_CARDS; _SYNTH_CARDS = sel; body, dropped = _clean(raw)` inside `one()`, which runs on a ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=6) (line 441). _SYNTH_CARDS is a MODULE-LEVEL GLOBAL read by _clean() at lines 403-404 at call time. Six threads race to overwrite it. Thread A's synthesis sentences are therefore validated against Thread B's premises — the 'NO NEW ENTITY' check (synthesis_contract.py) compares capitalised tokens against the WRONG subsection's premise blob, so legitimate sentences fail and are dropped. This is a plausible mechanism for 7 subsections being deleted down to nothing.
result.truncation_evidence:
TWO INDEPENDENT TRUNCATION MECHANISMS. Neither is "max_tokens=3000" as stated — that value never reaches the API.

=== MECHANISM 1: THE REAL CEILING IS 4096, NOT 3000 ===
cellcog_composer.py:429 passes `max_tokens=3000`. openrouter_client.py:1966-1970 silently overrides it:
    # Enforce minimum max_tokens so reasoning doesn't starve content.
    _min_tokens = int(os.getenv("PG_GLM5_MIN_MAX_TOKENS", "4096"))
    if body.get("max_tokens", 0) < _min_tokens:
        body["max_tokens"] = _min_tokens
GLM-5 is in _ALWAYS_REASON, so it hits this branch. logs/pg_cost_ledger.jsonl for the exact run (28 calls, 17:37:37 -> 17:42:11, matching report.md mtime 17:42:11.56 and the 28 OUTLINE subsections): 22/28 calls returned >3000 output tokens (proving 3000 was ignored) and 9/28 pinned at EXACTLY 4096 — finish_reason=length, cut off mid-generation.

The two surviving hard truncations, quoted verbatim:

L189, the ENTIRE and ONLY paragraph of "### Implications that follow from the mechanisms" — ends on the word "and":
   "...At the worker level, writing in the The Quarterly Journal of Economics in 2002, Bresnahan et al. show that IT-induced organizational changes shift workers from functional specialists to process generalists by enabling access to centralized databases. These findings jointly establish that technological integration requires fundamental process and"

L171, the last line of "### What the evidence establishes" — ends on the dangling word "Still":
   "The evidence establishes a strong empirical association between technological susceptibility and human capital factors. **[Established finding]** Still"

_clean() (composer.py:381-411) has NO terminal-punctuation check, so it emits the guillotined tail verbatim. "**[Established finding]** Still" is 3 words, so it slips under the `len(s.split()) > 8` gate threshold at line 403 and is kept without inspection.

=== MECHANISM 2 (DOMINANT): THE GATE SPLITS SENTENCES ON "et al." ===
cellcog_composer.py:391:  sents = re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', p)
The period in "et al." is a sentence boundary to this regex. 64 of 133 evidence cards (48%) have an attribution ending in "et al." (verified against outputs/evidence_cards.json). WRITE_PROMPT template A — "Writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, <AUTHORS> show that <finding>." — therefore ALWAYS bisects. Replayed the exact regex:

  input : "Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al. show that AI affects labor. Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al. report that understanding the impact of AI on jobs requires understanding prediction."
  output: [0] 'Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al.'   <- has "Writing in the" -> line 403 BYPASSES the gate -> KEPT
          [1] 'show that AI affects labor.'                                              <- 5 words, under threshold -> kept
          [2] 'Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al.'   <- BYPASSES the gate -> KEPT
          [3] 'report that understanding the impact of AI on jobs requires understanding prediction.'  <- 12w, NO source name -> SENT TO _gate_synthesis() -> DROPPED

42 such bisection sites exist in the report. The rendered wreckage:

(a) DOUBLED ATTRIBUTION — L59, first sentence of "### Prediction machines". The finding-half was gated out; both attribution-halves survived and were rejoined by `' '.join(good)` at line 410:
   "Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al. Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al. report that understanding the impact of AI on jobs requires understanding the relative roles of prediction and decision tasks."

(b) SUBJECTLESS PREDICATES — L121, "### Why large task gains coexist with weak aggregate effects". The attribution-halves were dropped, the bare verb-halves survived. Two headless sentences in a row:
   "...as economic factors like labor cost and demand elasticity also matter. show that the effects of IT on labor demand are larger when IT is combined with specific organizational investments. show that computer automation primarily substitutes for routine clerical and similar tasks but has limited scope for automating complex managerial and professional work."

(c) HEADLESS ATTRIBUTIONS — the finding was deleted, the citation stub was kept. Four of them:
   L101 (paragraph just STOPS on the attribution, nothing follows):
     "...This short-run theoretical divergence is complicated when viewing AI specifically as a prediction mechanism. Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al."
   L131: "While individual firms benefit from automation, broader regional labor markets suffer. Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al. However, at the regional level, the adoption of industrial robotics negatively impacts regional employment and wages."
   L133: "This economy-wide effect aligns with manufacturing-specific firm outcomes. Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al. These findings jointly establish that manufacturing bears the brunt of automation shocks..."
   L141: "This long-run economy-wide shift toward skilled labor aligns with firm-level productivity gains in specific sectors. Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al. **[Established finding]**"

(d) A PARAGRAPH THAT IS NOTHING BUT A TAG — L177, the second paragraph of "### Where the literature genuinely disagrees", in its entirety:
   "**[Unresolved]**"

(e) THE WHOLE SUBSECTION "### Where the literature genuinely disagrees" is 38 WORDS and names no disagreement:
   "Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Acemoglu and Restrepo show that the introduction of new tasks where labor has a comparative advantage reinstates labor, consistently increasing both labor share and labor demand. **[Established finding]**
    
    **[Unresolved]**"

(f) SEVEN SUBSECTIONS TRUNCATED TO ZERO. All 28 LLM calls succeeded (28 ledger rows, none with 0 output, max duration 90s vs 420s timeout) — so `except Exception: return job, '', []` never fired. _clean() deleted 100% of the prose in 7 of 28 subsections, and write_report():467-469 (`if not body: continue`) dropped their headings silently. Casualties: "How the Fourth Industrial Revolution differs from the previous three", "Why AI is a general-purpose technology rather than a single innovation", "Skill-biased technical change and its limits", "Exposure is not adoption, and adoption is not impact", "Evidence for reinstatement and new task creation", "Skill demand is being recomposed rather than simply raised", "Task-level productivity gains in controlled settings".
result.repetition_evidence:
COUNT: 135 attribution templates in 6,305 body words = ONE EVERY 46.7 WORDS, i.e. roughly every second sentence. There is effectively only ONE template; the three "variants" are the same string inverted.

  A.  "Writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, <AUTHORS> ..."      x 91
  B.  "<AUTHORS>, writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, ..."     x 37
  C.  "; writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, <AUTHORS> ..."    x  6   (semicolon splice)
                                                              -----
                                                                134  (+1 edge case = 135 total)

The verb slot has TWO fillers, covering 88% of all uses:
  "show that" x67 + "shows that" x12  = 79
  "report that" x34 + "reports that" x6 = 40
  everything else (find/note/argue/establish/demonstrate) = 16

PARAGRAPH DENSITY: 44 of 65 body paragraphs carry TWO OR MORE attributions. Three paragraphs carry FOUR. Only 5 paragraphs have none.

THE SAME STRING, VERBATIM, OVER AND OVER:
  x11  "Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Acemoglu and Restrepo"
  x11  "Writing in the The Quarterly Journal of Economics in 2002, Bresnahan et al."
  x10  "Writing in the Technological Forecasting and Social Change in 2017, Frey and Osborne"
  x 9  "Writing in the American Economic Review in 2018, Acemoglu and Restrepo"
  x 7  "Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2015, Autor"
  x 5  "Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al."

WORST OFFENDER — "### Healthcare, education, and the caring professions" (179 words, ZERO healthcare content). Frey & Osborne, four consecutive times, four different phrasings of the same paper:
  "Writing in the Technological Forecasting and Social Change in 2017, Frey and Osborne show that the probability of computerisation can be estimated for 702 detailed occupations using a Gaussian process classifier."
  "Frey and Osborne, writing in the Technological Forecasting and Social Change in 2017, report that a primary objective of their study is to analyze the number of jobs at risk from computerisation."
  "...writing in the Technological Forecasting and Social Change in 2017, Frey and Osborne show that they examine expected impacts of future computerisation on US labour market outcomes."
  "Writing in the Technological Forecasting and Social Change in 2017, Frey and Osborne show that their study investigates the relationship between an occupation's probability of computerisation and both wages and educational attainment."

Then the SAME FOUR SENTENCES are recycled almost word-for-word in "### What the evidence establishes" (L167-171), AGAIN in "### What the evidence cannot yet resolve" (L181-183), and AGAIN in "### Gaps the evidence itself exposes" (L193-195). Four different subsections, one paper, the same four findings.

L59 — the template printed TWICE IN A ROW inside one sentence:
  "Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al. Writing in the Journal of Economic Perspectives in 2019, Agrawal et al. report that..."

ROOT: WRITE_PROMPT rule 1 (cellcog_composer.py:295-297) mandates the exact shape ("in this exact shape"), and _fmt_cards() (line 350) reprints the frozen string per card with "ATTRIBUTION (use this exact wording)". Variety was engineered out of the system on purpose — the docstring (lines 9-13) says this is "the ONLY citation form that survives RACE's LLM cleaner". It survives, and it reads like a machine.

It also drags in grammar errors, because the string is "Writing in the {venue}" and the venue is not always article-ready:
  x15  "the The Quarterly Journal of Economics"
  x 1  "the The Journal of Economic History"
  x 7  "in the PLOS ONE in 2020"
  x 5  "in the Science in 2023"
result.root_cause:
ONE LINE CAUSES MOST OF IT: cellcog_composer.py:391 — `sents = re.split(r'(?<=[.!?])\s+', p)` — treats the period in "et al." as a sentence boundary.

48% of the evidence cards (64/133) carry an attribution string ending in "et al.", and WRITE_PROMPT rule 1 mandates the shape "Writing in the <JOURNAL> in <YEAR>, <AUTHORS> show that <finding>." Every such sentence is therefore bisected by _clean() into two pseudo-sentences with OPPOSITE fates:
  - the attribution half ("Writing in the Eurasian Business Review in 2021, Damioli et al.") contains "Writing in the", so line 403 BYPASSES the synthesis gate -> always kept;
  - the finding half ("show that ...") names no source and exceeds 8 words, so it is routed to _gate_synthesis() -> frequently deleted.
The gate then deletes roughly half the actual findings while religiously preserving their citations. `' '.join(good)` (line 410) welds the survivors back together, producing headless attributions, subjectless "show that..." clauses, doubled templates, and 7 subsections emptied to nothing (all 28 LLM calls succeeded — the cost ledger proves it — so `if not body: continue` at line 468 dropped those headings silently, with no error and no retry).

Three compounding faults on top:
1. max_tokens=3000 (line 429) never reaches the API — openrouter_client.py:1966-1970 raises it to a 4096 GLM-5 floor. 9 of 28 calls pinned at exactly 4096 and were cut off mid-sentence; _clean() has no terminal-punctuation check and emits the stump ("...requires fundamental process and", "**[Established finding]** Still").
2. _select() (lines 333-342) matches cards to subsections by naive lexical overlap on the H3 TITLE. For "How the Fourth Industrial Revolution differs from the previous three" the best card scores 2 and no card in the corpus discusses industrial revolutions — so it fed 10 irrelevant cards to a prompt that says "these are the ONLY facts you may state". The 4IR argument, which the composer's own comment (lines 229-231) identifies as worth ~11.5% of the score, was never writable. "Fourth Industrial Revolution" appears exactly twice in the document: in the hard-coded Abstract and in a heading. Zero times in prose.
3. `global _SYNTH_CARDS` (line 434) is mutated by 6 concurrent threads (ThreadPoolExecutor at line 441) and read by _clean() at call time — so a subsection's sentences can be validated against a DIFFERENT subsection's premises, causing valid synthesis to fail the no-new-entity check and be dropped.

And the reference list: there is none, and no code path can emit one. The assembly block (lines 449-472) is a hard-coded title + Abstract + OUTLINE loop, full stop. Worse, WRITE_PROMPT rule 1 forbids parenthetical years and _clean() line 408 (`PAREN_YEAR.sub('', s)`) deletes any that appear — so the report has zero markers, zero author-year citations, and zero bibliography. Every source exists only inside the one repeated prose template. The cards already carry doi/authors/venue/year; the data is right there and is simply never written out.
